questions.questionText:
Mwaka Hima sued Daima Tele in Milimani High Court Civil Case No. E304 of 2023 for special damages of Ksh. 35,000,000/= plus costs and interest. The Plaintiff conducted the matter in person whereas the Defendant was represented by the law firm of Sheria Bora & Company Advocates, where Mr. Sheria Bora is the sole proprietor.
Judgment was delivered on 8th September 2023, whereupon the Court dismissed the matter with costs to Daima Tele since, according to the Court, Mwaka Hima failed to prove his case on a balance of probabilities.
Conclusion of the matter notwithstanding, Mwaka Hima has taken issue with paragraph 5 of Daima Tele's Statement of Defence dated 4th July 2023 filed in the matter, which read as follows:
"5. The Defendant avers that the Plaintiff is not entitled to the sums claimed as alleged, or at all. The Defendant hastens to add that the alleged sums claimed by the Plaintiff were fraudulently siphoned by the Plaintiff from MwaHiDa Te Company Limited, which is a separate legal entity and, in any event, has not been sued in this matter. The Plaintiff is an unapologetic thief, conman and insensitive fraudster, who has tormented the Defendant, who is his co-director/shareholder in the company, hence, the Defendant is nonsuited to the Plaintiff."
Based on the foregoing paragraph, Mwaka Hima filed a fresh claim as follows:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE ENVIRONMENT AND LAND COURT AT NYERI
CIVIL CASE NO. E450 OF 2025
MWAKA HIMA.....PETITIONER
-VERSUS-
DAIMA TELE...1ST ACCUSED
SHERIA BORA...ACCUSED 2ND
MWAHIDATE COMPANY LIMITED....3RD ACCUSED
PLAINT
1. The Plaintiff is an adult of sound mind and disposition, a businessman based in Nyeri. The Plaintiff's address for purposes of this suit shall be Nyeri Business Centre, 3rd Floor, P.O. Box 100, Nyeri; email address mwakahima2023@gmail.com.
2. The 1st Defendant is a female adult and a businesswoman based in Nairobi. The 2nd Defendant is a male adult, an Advocate of the High Court of Kenya and the sole proprietor of Sheria Bora & Company Advocates, whose offices are situated in Kitale. The 2nd Defendant has been sued in his individual capacity for misadvising the 1st Plaintiff, who was his client in Milimani High Court Civil Case No. E304 of 2023 as the Defendant.
3. The 3rd Defendant is a limited liability company, duly incorporated as such under the Companies Act, Cap 486 of the Laws of Kenya, with its registered offices in Kwale.
4. The Plaintiff avers that in the 1st Defendant's paragraph 5 of his Statement of Defence dated 4th July 2023 filed in Milimani High Court Civil Case No. E304 of 2023, the 1st Defendant stated as follows of and concerning the Plaintiff:
"5. The Defendant avers that the Plaintiff is not entitled to the sums claimed as alleged, or at all. The Defendant hastens to add that the alleged sums claimed by the Plaintiff were fraudulently siphoned by the Plaintiff from MwaHiDaTe Company Limited, which is a separate legal entity and, in any event, has not been sued in this matter. The Plaintiff is an unapologetic thief, conman and insensitive fraudster, who has tormented the Defendant, who is his co-director/shareholder in the company, hence, the Defendant is nonsuited to the Plaintiff."
5. The Plaintiff contends that the said words, either in their natural or ordinary meaning, were meant or understood to mean that the Plaintiff:
i) is a thief;
ii) is a conman;
iii) is fraudulent;
iv) is dishonest;
v) lives off dishonest activities; and
vi) is untrustworthy and cannot be anyone's co-director/shareholder.
6. By the 1st Defendant's conduct aforestated, the Plaintiff has been subjected to ridicule and his reputation has been lowered in the eyes of the right-thinking members of the society.
7. The Plaintiff further avers that initially, he was a successful businessman in Nyeri and beyond, but upon the 1st Defendant's impugned paragraph, his businesses have suffered and he has lost a string of clientele.
8. There is no other suit pending between the parties herein.
REASONS WHEREFORE the Plaintiff prays for:
(a) KSh. 10,000,000/= for special damages for defamation.
DATED AT NYERI this 3rd September 2025.
Mwaka Hima
Plaintiff
DRAWN AND FILED BY:
Mwaka Hima
Nyeri Business Centre, 3rd Floor
P.O. Box 100 NYERI
Email address mwakahima2023@gmail.com.
TO BE SERVED UPON:
Daima Tele
NAIROBI
Sheria Bora
C/o Sheria Bora & Company
Advocates whose
KITALE
MwaHiDaTe Company Limited
KWALE

All the Defendants have approached you to enter appearance on their behalves and defend them in the matter.
(a) Analyse the Plaint as drawn and advise your clients on the defects thereto. (8 Marks)
(b) Upon entering appearance and filing the Statement of Defence, you raised a Notice of Preliminary Objection, which was upheld by the Court. Assuming you were the presiding Judge, draft the decision. (6 Marks)
(c) The additional information that you were given by your clients when you were taking instructions is that upon filing the matter, Mwaka Hima called Daima Tele's estranged wife and maliciously effected service of the Pleadings and Summons to Enter Appearance on her so that Daima Tele does not come to learn of the proceedings. For Sheria Bora, he effected court process on the Laws Society of Kenya headquarters in Nairobi. He later caused an advert to be published in the Daily Nation, notifying the company of the existence of the proceedings. Advise your clients whether or not Mwaka Hima followed the right procedure. (6 Marks)

AB sued KUTDA Limited and obtained judgment in the sum of Ksh. 14,984,000/= in the Chief Magistrates court sitting at Machakos in CMCCC E023 of 2020. AB instructed K & K Auctioneers to attach all moveable properties belonging to the Judgement Debtor. The Auctioneers proclaimed goods belonging to Haci Trading Company Limited. Upon proclamation, Haci Trading Company Limited contemporaneously filed a Notice of Objection and Notice of Motion dated 15th May 2024 with a supporting affidavit. Haci Trading Company Limited attached to the Notice of Objection a Certificate of Change of name from Haci Warehousing Limited to its current name; invoices, delivery notes and receipts for the supply of computers, office equipment and accessories; and Registration Certificates for the motor vehicles that had been proclaimed. In the Motion, Haci Trading Company Limited prayed for a permanent injunction restraining the Decree Holder and K & K Auctioneers either by themselves, their servants, employees and/or assigns from attaching, selling by way of public auction or any other way the moveable property listed in the proclamation issued to the Objector by the Auctioneer pursuant to a warrant of attachment of moveable property in execution of a decree for money. In the supporting affidavit., it was averred that the goods which had been proclaimed did not belong to the Judgment Debtor, but to the Objector. The application was opposed on the grounds that the objection was frivolous, vexatious and an outright abuse of the court process, and was based on falsehood to deny the decree-holder the fruits of his judgment. The decree holder averred that the summons to enter appearance were received by the same person who had sworn the supporting affidavit, and that the Judgement Debtor shared the same address with the Objector. It was further contended that the attached goods belonged to the Judgment Debtor and not the Objector, who had wholly failed to establish title and ownership to the goods and that the goods belonged to the Judgment Debtor as the holding company of the Objector. On the basis of the materials placed before the court, the parties filed written submissions and the matter is pending for ruling before the trial Magistrate.
a) Analyse the procedural defects in the above facts. (5 marks)
b) In its ruling delivered on 25th July 2024, Hon C Mheshimiwa dismissed the objection proceedings on the ground that even though the Objector was not a party to the suit, the Defendant Judgment Debtor held the majority shares in the Objector. Furthermore, the person who deponed the supporting affidavit was also the one who had received the summons to enter appearance in the suit on behalf of the Judgment Debtor. There was also evidence to the effect that the Objector had changed its name to Chai Trading Company Ltd after the institution of the suit. Therefore, both the Judgement Debtor and the Objector shared the same premises and, in the circumstances, the Objector and the Judgement Debtor were the same. Although the Objector had a legal and/or equitable interest in the attached goods, it was one and the same company as the Judgment Debtor and the Decree Holder rightly attached the property in question. The Objector has instructed you to appeal against the said ruling. Draft the appropriate pleading you intend to file. (5 Marks)

Christabel got a judgment and decree against Kim in Kikuyu Law Courts Chief Magistrates Civil Case No. 70 of 2023. The decree was for a sum of six million shillings (Ksh. 6,000,000). Christabel made several attempts to execute the said decree in vain. At some point, she took out proceedings to commit Kim to civil jail, as a result of which the Judgment Debtor served civil jail for the entire period of six (6) months, but came out of jail without paying the decretal sum. Therefore, Christabel felt that the further commitment of Kim to civil jail would be tantamount to throwing good money after bad money. When she was just about to give up on the prospects of ever recovering her money, she got a tip off from a friend, who works at the National Transport and Safety Authority (NTSA) that Kim had a motor vehicle registered under his name. An official search at the Registry revealed that, indeed, Motor Vehicle Registration No. KBH 851P was registered in the name of Kim. Therefore, Christabel instructed an auctioneer to trace, proclaim, attach and sell motor vehicle KBH 851P for purposes of recovering at least part of the decretal sum. Pursuant to this instruction, on 30th October 2025, armed with the warrants of attachment obtained from court, the auctioneer found the motor vehicle KBH 851P parked outside Stand Bar at Kamirithu shopping centre. He clamped it and towed it to his yard ready for auction. At that juncture, Leonard, who was in possession of the motor vehicle, reported it stolen. The police tracked the vehicle to the auctioneer's yard, where the auctioneer narrated the circumstances under which the motor vehicle had been attached and towed. For his part, Leonard explained that he had purchased the motor vehicle from Kim twelve months prior to the incident and had paid the full purchase price for it. He even produced the sale agreement and the payment details, indicating that what was outstanding was the formal transfer. Having heard the parties, the police advised Leonard that this was not an appropriate case for criminal investigations and prosecution. The best remedy lay in a civil action. Leonard comes to your office of Wakili Mnoma Advocates LLP crestfallen. He asks you to take all measures known to the law to ensure that his vehicle is not auctioned. He has paid the deposit of your legal fees as discussed. Draft the necessary documents to safeguard Leonard's interests. (10 marks)

QUE Limited, a company engaged in construction business, is one of the oldest clients in the Law Firm you have been working at as an associate. QUE Limited offices are located at Tihika, Kiambu County. On 13th June 2024, QUE Limited won a tender to construct a Four Storey building at Hela Estate in Thika. Instead of undertaking the construction, QUE Limited Sub-Contracted the work to EON Limited under a sub-contractor agreement. As per the terms of that agreement, EON Limited was to hire all the laborers, insure them as required under the law and take responsibility for any claims that would arise in relation to the construction. One Monday morning, Kijana, who was a foreman in the construction site stumbled and fell in a manhole at the construction site. He broke his leg and sustained serious bruises on his face. When he sought help from the project manager, he discovered that EON Limited had not taken insurance cover for the laborers on site. He was forced to foot the hospital bills. Therefore, upon recovery, he instructed Wakili Mjanja & Co Advocates to file a suit against QUE Limited, seeking damages for the losses he had suffered. The suit was filed and pleadings served upon QUE Limited, who has forwarded the case to you with instructions to defend it and ensure that based on the sub-contractor agreement entered between QUE Limited and EON Limited, EON Limited is held responsible. Explain the process that will be taken to add EON Limited as a party to the suit on the facts of this case. (10 Marks)

In the course of your work as an associate at JQS Law LLP, you have received instructions from one of the law firm's clients, Jenga Microfinance Ltd, to institute a suit against one Madoya Mzee, who has defaulted in the repayment of a loan advanced to him by Jenga Microfinance Ltd. You have drafted and filed the plaint. Paragraph 3 of the Plaint reads as follows:
On or about 20th July 2024, the Defendant visited the Plaintiffs offices and applied for a loan of Kshs. 250,000.00/-. The Plaintiff granted the Defendant the loan on conditions that the same was to be repaid within 24 months and the loan would attract a monthly interest of 1%.
Two days after filing the suit, your client calls you and points out fundamental errors in the plaint as follows:
i. The loan was advanced on 30th July 2023 and not 20th July 2024 as stated in the plaint.
ii. The loan was to be repaid within 12 months and not 24 months.
iii. You had failed to include a further condition that in default of payment within 12 months, the Defendant would attach and sell the Plaintiffs motor vehicle Reg. No. KYD1115, which the Defendant had provided as security.
a. Proceed to amend paragraph 3 of the Plaint to reflect the new information shared by the client. (3 Marks)
b. Do you require leave of the court to amend the Plaint in this case? Explain the circumstances under which leave of the court will be required to amend pleadings to justify your answer. (3 Marks)
c. Upon service of the Pleadings, the Defendant has filed an application seeking to have the suit struck out on the ground that the parties were not correctly described. It is also argued that there was no averment that no other suit was pending between the parties. Citing relevant authorities, discuss the principles that will guide the court when determining the Defendant's applications for striking out the suit. (4 Marks)

Richard owns Nakuru Block 13/34 which he acquired in 1980. To access the main road from his land he has to pass through Abbey's land, a small stretch that Abbey had permitted him to use. In January 2012, Abbey died and subsequently Paddy, his son and the only heir, sold the land to Peterson in 2020. In January 2021, Peterson accused Richard of trespass and warned him to keep off his land. Peterson has initiated the process to fence off the land.
a) In context of conveyancing, explain the property right created in this case. (2 marks)
b) With aid of relevant law, discuss the key ingredients necessary for recognition of the issue identified in (a) above. (5 marks)
c) Advice Richard on the appropriate legal action he can take against Peterson, if any. (3 marks)

instructions: Answer FIVE (5) questions, including question ONE, which is COMPULSORY. Question ONE carries 20 Marks. All other questions carry 10 Marks each. Attempt each question in the space provided. Answers MUST be supported by relevant statutory provisions and case law where required.

questions.questionText:
On 4th June 2025, following a report by Afande Mkubwa at the Tewa Police Station in Nairobi, officers from the Serious Crimes Unit (SCU) traced and arrested Brian at his home, over 320 kilometers from the capital city. The complaint by Afande Mkubwa, was to the effect that Brian had made a post on his Facebook Account that Afande Mkubwa was incorrigibly corrupt, and that he received loots from bribes collected by traffic police officers across the country. At the time of Afande Mkubwa's complaint, the post had garnered over 100,000 likes, 70,000 comments and had been shared at least 2,000 times. Upon arrest, the SCU officers transported Brian overnight and booked him at the Tewa Police Station, where PC Mikani and PC Kumwana were the officers on cell sentry duties for the night. It later emerged that on the night of 8th June 2025, Chief Inspector (CIP) Ruben, in a bid to please Afande Mkubwa, organized with PC Kumwana to 'teach Brian a lesson of his life for soiling the good name of Afande Mkubwa'. PC Kumwana enlisted the services of three inmates, Kagula, Jama and Labomba, who were in the cell to join him and PC Mikani to discipline Brian. PC Kumwana even smuggled a bottle of Kenya Cane 'Quota' to motivate the inmates in the exercise. Brian died from the violence meted on him by the officers and the inmates. Investigations by the independent government authority established that he had succumbed on the night 8th June 2025 from the physical trauma inflicted upon him. On the basis of the authority's findings, the Director of Public Prosecutions (DPP) accordingly made the decision to charge CIP Ruben, PC Mikani, PC Kumwana, Kagula, Jama and Labomba for the unlawful killing of Brian. However, Afande Mkubwa was not charged. Unhappy with the exclusion of Afande Mkubwa from the charges, twenty Petitioners moved to the High Court seeking, inter alia, a mandatory injunction to compel the DPP to also prefer charge against Afande Mkubwa for the death of Brian, arguing that the abduction and subsequent death of Brian was initiated by the unlawful complaint by Afande Mkubwa. In the alternative, the Petitioners have sought leave to file private prosecution against Afande Mkubwa.
(a) Comment on the question of jurisdiction with respect to the prayer for private prosecution. (2 marks)
(b) Citing relevant legislative provisions and/or case law, explain at least three (3) essential conditions for the grant of leave to undertake private prosecution. (6 marks)
(c) Assuming that leave for private prosecution was granted, and the Petitioners appointed you as their Private Prosecutor, draft the charge against Afande Mkubwa. (6 marks)
(d) Labomba has approached the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions through his advocates with an offer to become a witness for the State in the case against the rest of his co-accused persons. Explain three (3) essential conditions for the admissibility of the potential plea agreement, noting to cite relevant legislative provisions and or case law. (6 marks)

Ali is a member of a Nairobi-based Men's Group called, "Twaweza". The members tasked him to look for land within Nakuru and its environs that they could buy for speculation and sub-division. Pursuant to these instructions, Ali contacted his friend, Kim, a known land broker to assist him. In due course, Kim was informed by a friend, Dalali, that he had come by a parcel whose owner was seriously looking for a buyer. Dalali told Kim that a shopkeeper called Dukawala had a copy of the land title given to him by the land owner, John Mahangaiko, with the instruction to source for a buyer. This copy was used by Ali to establish the location and ownership of the parcel from the Land Registry. When Kim, Dalali and Ali accompanied by two other members of Twaweza visited the parcel, they found onions growing on the farm that Dalali indicated belonged to an unidentified person who had leased it. Having liked the parcel, members of Twaweza approved commencement of negotiations. Ali and some representatives of group together with Kim then met Dukawala, who contacted a person he identified as Mahangaiko (the landowner), on phone. Ali and representatives of the group eventually meet with that person in Nairobi. The purchase price was settled at three million, five hundred thousand (Ksh. 3,500,000) and a deal was sealed with the exchange of documents. Ali also paid Kim, Dalali and Dukawala the brokerage fees on behalf of Twaweza. Later, Ali and his team visited the land and found someone harvesting the onions. On asking him to rush the harvest so that they could start the process of subdivision, the man told them that he was an employee of John Mahangaiko, and to his knowledge, the land was not for sale. The man then called the owner, who arrived in two hours and identified himself as John Mahangaiko, a resident of Nyahururu. When the matter was reported to the police, the Directorate of Criminal Investigation (DCI) was able to track the phone details of the alleged seller, eventually leading to the arrest of Peter Mjanja, who had passed off as John Mahangaiko. Mjanja was then charged alongside Dalali and Dukawala. The matter was initially handled by Hon. Mkali, who took the evidence of four witnesses before he was transferred to another station. Hon. Jaber then took over the case, but inadvertently failed to comply with the provisions of section 200 of the CPC (which allows the accused to choose whether to continue with the recorded evidence or seek recall of witnesses when a judicial officer ceases to hear the case). Eventually, all the three accused persons were convicted for conspiracy to defraud and each is sentenced to 3 years' imprisonment. Peter Mjanja was also convicted of several other charges including forgery and obtaining money by false pretence. Dalali and Dukawala have appealed the decision to the High Court in Nakuru.
(a) Given the circumstances of this case, what factors will the court take into consideration in determining the appeal? (5 marks)
(b) The High Court eventually decides to order a retrial in this case. What factors influence the order for a retrial? (5 marks)

Mlagui Mzae is an avid blogger, who comments on emerging political issues in the country through his social media handle "Mkenya Halisi." On 25th June 2025, at the height of protests in the country in commemoration of the 25th June 2024 "Finance Bill" Protests, Mlagui posted information on his "X" and "Facebook" handles to the effect that he was being trailed by an unmarked car, which had been parked outside his residence in Wangige Town within Kiambu County, before his accounts become inactive. Concerned for his welfare, his parents decided to check on him the following day at his apartment. They found the door open and his personal items including his clothes strewn all over the floor. Mlagui's neighbors informed them that they had seen Mlagui being bundled into an unmarked car by persons who identified themselves as police officers. The parents checked with all the police stations in the area, but did not find him and the police denied any knowledge of his arrest. The search was extended to police stations in neighboring Nairobi County but to no avail. It is now more than three weeks since Mlagui disappeared and the parents are apprehensive of his safety. His father, Hakim Mzae has approached you seeking help to find his son. Draft the appropriate pleadings for filing in court to secure his release. (10 Marks)

A. Atur was arrested and presented before the Chief Magistrate's Court at Kisumu following a complaint by Adiso that Atur had waylaid her on her way from visiting Nyangi, and pointed a knife at her as he took off with her mobile phone. He was charged with the offence of robbery with violence contrary to section 295 as read with section 296 (2) of the Penal Code. Explain possible challenges to the charge considering the jurisprudential position on the offence. (4 marks)
B. Utuho Gift was attacked by security guards contracted by Quick Solutions Mining Limited located at Kanziko Sub-County, Kitui County. This happened on 2nd September 2025 at around 1600 Hours. The attackers claimed that Utuho got access to the Company's premises by jumping over the fence. The guard suspected him of the recent theft of the Company's raw limestone from the Company's stores. After the arrest, Utuho was tied with ropes around the neck and the upper and lower limbs, and then locked in the guard's changing room. This was witnessed by Junia John, an 8-year-old grade 3 pupil at Kanziko Primary School. At 1830 Hours, the night-shift security guards reported for work. However, they refused to take over the duties without the Director of the Company, Butt Charles, being informed of the arrest and assault of Utuho by the day-shift guards. When he was informed, the Director asked the guards to share photos of the arrested suspect. A day-shift guard who had a smart phone then took and shared the suspect's pictures through WhatsApp. Shortly after, the Director instructed the guards to 'please release the suspect.' After Utuho, who was barely conscious and could hardly walk, was untied, he staggered about 10 meters from the Company entrance and collapsed dead. Police upon investigations established the cause of death to be blunt force trauma on the head. The two day-shift guards, Benson Jayt and Collins Edu, were arrested and have been charged with Murder contrary to section 203 as read with section 204 of the Penal Code. The Director, on the hand, had been charged with Conspiracy to commit a felony contrary to section 393 of the Penal Code, but the DPP, disallowed the charge. The Director, was the DPP's class mate at the University and at Kenya School of Law. The family of the deceased (Utuho Gift) has come to you, as a renowned criminal advocate practicing in Kitui township, to help in pursuit of justice for their kin. Assuming Junia John has been called to record his statement at Kanziko Police station, and positively identified the two security guards, illustrate to your clients the procedure of presenting his (Junia John) evidence before the trial court. (6 marks)

Around 11 p.m. on 14th June 2025, John, a taxi driver registered with Uber®, received a random mobile callup from a client, Mary, who requested to be taken to her house in Ruaka. That was the last time Mary was seen alive. After three days, her body was discovered by a herdsman in Pondamali, Nakuru, next to an abandoned house. Post mortem examination revealed that she had been raped and strangled. During police investigation, John was arrested, but maintained that he dropped the client at her destination. He was later released after data from Uber® confirmed his assertion showing that he dropped a client at Ruaka around 11:30p.m. At the time, it was raining heavily and the CCTV camera at the apartment was not working as a result of a power blackout. The gate and the vehicles that usually parked outside the apartment were unmanned that night. Investigations revealed that Mary had been out with Kevo, her secret lover, that day. The two had taken separate taxis to and from the venue of their outing. Kevo's wife confirmed that had he arrived home around midnight the night of 14th June 2025. Text messages on Mary's phone, which was recovered from her pocket, showed she had been chatting with her boyfriend, Mato, a Military Officer based in Manda, Lamu. Mary and Mato had been together since leaving High School. Their families were close and they had been due to marry in December 2025. Records from Manda Base showed that Mato was away on Rest and Recreation in Nairobi. From the retrieved texts, it was discovered that Mato had asked Mary for the details of the taxi that was dropping her home. His last WhatsApp post read "Siwezi kubali kugongewa" (meaning in this context, I cannot accept a partner to cheat on me). His phone triangulation placed him within Eastleigh Airbase, his usual place of residence.
(a) The investigating officer has forwarded the file to the ODPP for perusal and advise. The ODPP proposes a public inquest be conducted. Explain the circumstances under which the inquest may be held and whether it is appropriate to the circumstances of this case. (5 Marks)
(b) Outline the procedure of conducting an inquest. (5 Marks)

When Skwota and Mumiliki's families appeared before the Environment and Land Court on a protracted dispute over a 100-acre farm, the Court issued orders to evict Skwota from the farm within 90 days. Skwota, who has invested heavily in the land and operates a luxury hotel therein, fears that the eviction would result in huge losses running into millions for him. To forestall this, he secures the services of a hit-man, Jambazi, to kill Mumiliki. Jambazi assembles a team, financed by Skwota through MPESA remittances. The team goes in search for Mumiliki. In the process, they encounter Mumiliki's Farm Manager, who shares with them the history of the land and a copy of the eviction order. Jambazi's conscience is pricked and he calls off the hit on Mumiliki. He is then facilitated by Mumiliki's Farm Manager to report the matter to the DCI. Together, Jambazi and his team record their statement with the police. Jambazi also surrenders his phone, which has recordings of their conversations with Skwota in Kinywe language.
(a) You are the Prosecution Counsel in the matter. The Directorate of Criminal Investigations has asked for assistance in the investigations. Elaborate the legal basis and what key features of the investigations you would consider crucial in this case? (8 marks)
(b) The prosecution decides to charge Skwota. In the course of pre-trial, the prosecution learns that two of Jambazi's accomplices have sworn separate affidavits recanting their statements. What are the options available for the Prosecution? (2 marks)

instructions: Answer FIVE (5) questions, including question ONE, which is COMPULSORY. Question ONE carries 20 Marks. All other questions carry 10 Marks each. Attempt each question in the space provided. Answers MUST be supported by relevant statutory provisions and case law where required.

questions.questionText:
Mr. Burrows, a senior bachelor, owned various movable and immovable assets. He sired a son out of wedlock with Betty when they were in college. He took responsibility then, named the son Lenny Peters Burrows. His name appeared as the father in the child's birth certificate. He parted ways with Betty seven years after college upon finding her red-handed with another man. He had left her with Lenny, indicating that he did not wish to see either of them again. Due to the heartbreak, Burrows decided never to marry. A few years later when Lenny was set to join a secondary school, having passed the primary school national examinations with flying colors, Betty, who had lost her job, requested Burrows to help her pay Lenny's secondary school fees. Burrows accepted to help on condition that none of them would come near him, and for the four years Lenny was in school, Burrows paid the requisite fees directly to the school, but had no physical contact with him or his mother Betty. When Lenny subsequently passed his secondary school and was admitted to study Medicine at the University of Mount Pleasant, Betty again approached Burrows for financial help. He obliged and gave her money to cater for the admission and first semester fees, but insisted that Lenny should apply for the government loan and bursary moving forward. Thereafter, he stopped paying Lenny's fees. However, he decided to nominate Lenny as the next of kin in Manhattan Savings and Credit Co-operative Society, where he was a member with 1000 shares. Simultaneously, Burrows made a Will to put his house in order. Although Burrows never married, he had a long-term relationship with a lady called Lisa that commenced in 2015. Burrows supported Lisa financially and also paid school fees for her daughter Marie. Lisa accompanied him in all family functions, where he introduced her as his personal assistant. Although she never lived with him in the same house continuously, she usually spent weekends in his house and they also went on holidays together. He had financed her to open a signature perfume shop in Brooklyn Mall for which she was registered as the sole proprietor trading as B&L Signature Perfumes. Together, they also jointly owned a sectional property title as tenants in common in apartment number 1401 in same mall. According to Burrow's will, he bequeathed his property as follows:
i. Bungalow in Victoria Lane - to my niece Patience.
ii. Town house in Bridgerton - to my nephew Phil.
iii. Money in the bank account at Preston Bank - to my sister Emily.
iv. 50 Acre piece of land in Whispering Hills - to Garden of Eden Charitable organization.
v. BMW X5 to my friend and confidant to Mr. Crawford.
vi. Suits and clothing - to be shared between my two brothers.
vii. Household furniture, cutlery and related items - to my P.A. Lisa.
viii. Shares in Manhattan Savings & Credit Co-operative Society - to Bradley.
ix. Apartment No. 1401 Brooklyn Mall to Neil.
x. Investment in B&L Signature Perfumes - to my father Fredrick.
The executor of the will indicated as Mr. Crawford. The will was duly witnessed. Last year, Mr. Burrows was travelling in a helicopter, which crashed at Whispering Hills Forest. All on board died. He was survived by his father Fredrick, his two brothers Neil and Bradley and his twin sister Emily. After the burial, his lawyer called the family for the reading of the will.
In light of the above narrative, consider the following scenarios:
a) Lenny Peters Burrows, as his Identity card reads, is in third year of medical school. He gets wind of the will and believes that he is entitled to inherit the entire, if not the biggest, portion of his deceased father's estate. He has approached Legal Aid Centre where you work and the file has been allocated to you. With the help of statutory provisions and case law, discuss Lenny's options in relation to his claim. (5 marks)
b) On the other hand, Lisa feels that she was more than a girlfriend as she cooked, cleaned and performed wifely duties for the deceased. She considered herself a wife and according to her, household effects are nothing compared to the vast estate. She has filed a case laying a claim to a bigger share of inheritance as a wife of the deceased. You are a Court researcher attached to Justice Atkins and you have been asked to research on the matter. Evaluate Lisa's case based on the existing legal position. (5 marks)
c) In the meantime, Fredrick has threatened to chase Lisa from B&L Signature Perfumes business as not only was it bequeathed to him, but also the B in B&L stands for Burrows. On the other hand, Neil has given Lisa 30 days' notice to vacate Apartment 1401. Lisa has in return written a letter to Neil telling him that since her name is in the title deed, it wholly belongs to her, since an apartment cannot be divided into two portions. Analyze the legal position of the bequests given to Fredrick and Neil in light of these arguments. (4 marks)
d) Brad and Lenny got into an altercation regarding Manhattan Savings and Credit Co-operative Society. You are a pupil at the Public Trustee offices and they have jointly come to you for advice on this bequest. Write a short opinion on the advice you would offer to the parties. (3 marks)
e) Mr. Crawford has approached your firm to initiate the succession process. Identify the type of petition you will file in court, distinguishing it from the application you would have filed had Burrows not left a will. (3 marks)

Ambrose and Winnie are long-term business partners with Winnie having been the sole supplier of beef to Ambrose's chain of supermarkets since 2005. In March 2024, Ambrose was taken ill and, despite being flown abroad for very specialized medical attention, he succumbed to what was later diagnosed as an acute case of lymphoma. By the time of his death, the supermarket owed Winnie a sum of two million, five hundred thousand (Kshs 2,500,000/=). After the funeral, Winnie discussed her business situation with Ambrose's widow, Beryl, who promised to start the process of administration of her deceased husband's estate to enable the family to get the legal basis to settle the debts owed by the supermarkets. Despite a number of follow-ups, Beryl does not commence the process, leaving Winnie very frustrated. It becomes apparent to Winnie that Beryl is not keen on getting the process done. Winnie seeks your legal advice on what options she has available. Relying on relevant legal provisions as may have been interpreted by courts, render a concise legal opinion noting to explain the step-by-step process involved. (10 marks)

Scott is a foreign national who owns an apartment in Kilimani Nairobi, a maisonette in London, two Toyota cars in Nairobi, a Mercedes Benz in London, an account at Equity Bank Nairobi and another account in Absa Bank London. He died in 2024, leaving a will in London in which he bequeathed all his assets to his two sons in the UK. Scott had a Kenyan family and was married to Tabitha with whom they had a daughter. They used to stay at the Kilimani Apartment as their matrimonial home. After his death, the sons in UK reiterated that they are the only lawful heirs as per the will. Therefore, Tabitha is afraid that she may be disinherited and that her daughter may not have a home.
a) Advise Tabitha on the key legal considerations in applying for grant with regard to Scott's movable and immovable assets. (6 Marks)
b) Formulate the possible outcome on distribution of Scotts estate. (4 Marks)

John died intestate on 5 August 2021. He left behind two wives, Jane, the first and Sabina, the second. Jane's children Kelly and Nelly are all adults, while Sabina's children Kelvin and Calvin are still school going. Following his death, Kelly who is an advocate together with her mother, Jane applied for letters of administration on 3rd August 2025 without the knowledge of Sabina and her children. Sabina came to know about it when her friend, who works at the Government Printer, sent her a copy of the Gazette Notice of the Magistrates' court in Kandara, Succession Cause No. E055 of 2025 relating to the intended appointment of Kelly and Jane as proposed administrators of John's estate. Sabina rushes to your office with a copy of the gazette notice. She has been advised that unless an urgent intervention is made, she, together with her children, risk being rendered destitute. Already, her children have been sent away from school despite their father having left behind substantial sums of monies in his bank accounts, details of which Sabina is privy to.
Based on the factual scenario:
a) Draft an appropriate application to be filed in court on behalf of Sabina and her children to address her concerns. (6 marks)
b) Assuming that the parties eventually agree, and joint administrators that are appointed to manage the estate, reach out to you for advice. Then before the confirmation of the Grant, Sabina discovers that Jane and Kelly have, since the death of John, been collecting rent from rental houses that the deceased owns in Nairobi. The rental income from the estate has neither been disclosed nor accounted for. Advise Sabina on the remedies (if any) that she may have in law against Jane and Kelly. (4 marks)

Allan graduated from the University of Nairobi in 1998 with a Bachelor of Civil and Structural Engineering. In 1999, he tied the knot with his longtime university lovebird, Jerusha, at the Holy Mountain of Zion Church. The union was blessed with four children, namely, Anne, Jemimah, Maurice and Allan Junior. Sometimes in 2020, Allan made a work-related visit to Saudi Arabia during that year's Islamic pilgrimage to the City of Mecca. There, he was so overawed by the sea of the humanity with their resplendent white outfits that seemed to spread over every inch of the city. From that point, Allan felt a very strong urge to become a Muslim and at some point in his life, to make a pilgrimage to the holy City. When he arrived back in Kenya, Allan informed his family of his attraction to Islam and despite their opposition, he converted to the faith even though the other members of his family did not. He then married Zubeida, a Muslim, with whom he sired two children, Mohamed and Nasri. As he drove to work on the morning of 13th March 2025, while at South C, Nairobi, Allan lost control of his car, which rolled multiple times, killing him instantly. He was laid to rest at Kariorkor Muslim Cemetery after the adhuhur prayers. After the burial, the family became entangled in a dispute in regards to the distribution of his estate. Both Jerusha and Zubeida have disputed the validity of each other's marriage to the late Allan. The matter has been referred to Lilly & Lilly Advocates LLP where you are presently interning. The Managing Partner has marked the file for your attention seeking that you:
a) Discuss with the client the legal rights of the children in regards to the distribution of Allan's estate. (4 marks)
b) Examine the extent of rights to Allan's estate for both Jerusha and Zubeida. (6 marks)

Mr. Katana's father, Charo, died two years ago. He did not leave a will, but it was understood by the family that his first-born son, Rashid, would ensure that his property is distributed between Rashid himself, his widow Amina and his children Maria, Mwadime and Katana according to the law. Rashid obtained letters of administration and began the process of distributing the estate of his father. Unfortunately, Rashid became suddenly ill and passed away while the process of distribution was still ongoing. By the time of his death, Rashid had not yet facilitated the transmission of the titles of properties to the beneficiaries. Thus, Mr. Katana feels that the responsibility of settling his father's estate now rests with him. He approaches you, a well-known family lawyer, to advise him on the steps he should take to complete the process of administration. Advise him on the following:
a) Whether it is possible for him to take over and complete the administration of the estate. (5 marks)
b) What process he ought to take to get authority to administer the estate. (5 marks)
topics: Letters of Administration de Bonis Non

instructions: Answer FIVE (5) questions, including question ONE, which is COMPULSORY. Question ONE carries 20 Marks. All other questions carry 10 Marks each. Attempt each question in the space provided. Answers MUST be supported by relevant statutory provisions and case law where required.

questions.questionText:
A. Study the following policy statements and draft penal provisions for each.
i) To provide a general penalty provision for a maximum penalty of three months' imprisonment or a fine of one hundred thousand or both the imprisonment and the fine for any offence committed and for which no penalty has been specifically provided. (2 marks)
ii) To provide a maximum punishment of one million shillings or imprisonment for five years or both the fine and imprisonment for persons in positions of authority in bodies corporate. Provided that such a person had knowledge of the commission of the offence and failed to take reasonable steps to stop the commission of the offence. And that prosecution of such persons shall not be dependent on conviction of the body corporate itself. (4 marks)
iii) A person required to provide any information under the Act but knowingly provides false or misleading information will be punished by a maximum fine of five hundred thousand shillings. (2 marks)
B. Re-write the following sentences in active voice.
i) The Artificial Intelligence Bill, 2025 and the Artificial Intelligence Policy 2025 were approved by the Cabinet on 10th December 2025. (1 mark)
ii) On 6th August 2025, the demand letter from Wake's advocate was received by the University of Akili Mingi. (1 mark)
iii) For the reasons that the application was too general and did not disclose any specific and urgent threat to her right to liberty, the applicant was not granted anticipatory bail by the court. (1 mark)
iv) The Magistrate's ruling on bail and bond terms was appealed on 20th May 2025 by the accused person to the High Court. (1 mark)
C. Mzee Matata and Mwalimu Mkuu have been good friends for a very long time. Mwalimu Mkuu retired in the year 2022 and ventured into farming. In order to start meaningful farming, Mwalimu Mkuu requested Mzee Matata to loan him Ksh. 250,000/ to boost his savings. Mzee Matata acquiesced to the request and the two friends entered into an agreement on 20th May 2023. Mzee Matata sent the amount of Ksh. 250,000/ to Mwalimu Mkuu via MPesa the same day they signed the agreement. The two friends agreed that the loan amount together with an interest of Ksh. 20,000/ was to be repaid in five equal quarterly instalments, commencing 1st January 2024. Whereas Mwalimu Mkuu repaid the first two instalments, he failed to repay subsequent instalments. Despite numerous reminders by Mzee Matata, the balance of the loan has not been repaid. This has greatly strained the good relationship the two friends had always enjoyed. Mzee Matata has approached your law firm for assistance to recover the outstanding amount from Mwalimu Mkuu. Prepare the appropriate document. (8 marks)

In August 2023, the Ministry of Education (MoE) directed the Kenya National Examinations Council (KNEC) to roll out a refreshed digital workflow for Kenya Primary School Education Assessment (KPSEA) for grade 6 assessment, placement and appeals. The directive followed an incident where a primary school circulated a spreadsheet of grade six scores and names on a messaging app, prompting parents to complain and the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC) to issue warnings. Determined to prevent a repeat, the MoE now requires a single written instrument that comprehensively governs the collection, use, sharing, storage, retention, security, cross-border transfers and breach handling of KPSEA grade 6 data across the entire workflow. Within this framework, institutional roles are fixed: the MoE is the data controller and custodian of the instrument; KNEC acts as the joint controller, and public and private primary schools function as data processors. The lawful basis for processing the data is expressly limited to what is necessary for a task carried out in the public interest and to comply with a legal obligation. Because the processing concerns children's data, parent/guardian consent is required, and age verification must occur at grade 6 registration to ensure consent is valid and attributable. All identifiable learner data should be retained for six years from completion of grade 6 and then de-identified. No personal data may be transferred outside Kenya, unless there is an ODPC recognized adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards in place and each arrangement must be recorded. An adequacy decision is a formal determination by the ODPC that a destination country/territory/organization ensures an adequate level of data protection. To safeguard confidentiality, integrity and availability, the instrument must impose appropriate technical and organizational security measures and set strict breach notification timelines. Any processor must notify the controller within 48 hours of a personal data breach, and the controller must notify the ODPC within 72 hours and affected parents/guardians be informed without undue delay where there is a real risk of harm. The institutions that are data processors or data controllers are required to appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO). This will be the central person to communicate with in relation to matters of data protection. Where ICT vendors and cloud hosts are engaged, they will be treated as processors and are bound by a written Data Processing Agreement (DPA) that includes audit rights. Throughout implementation and communication, the instrument must guarantee English/Kiswahili parity and ensure reasonable accommodation for learners with disabilities. This will ensure that access to information and processes is equitable for all grade 6 learners and their families. Working only with the given facts, draft the appropriate document that the Ministry of Education would issue to set the rules and procedures for handling KPSEA grade 6 learners' data across assessment, placement and appeals. Use clear numbered sub-sections and clauses. (10 marks)

The County Government of Nairobi has noted with concern that there are a number of stray domestic animals freely roaming in the capital city posing danger to residents and their property and even causing road accidents when they are on, or along the roads. You have been approached as an expert legislative drafter with the following policy directions.
a) No cows, goats, sheep or Bulls permitted to roam freely in the city.
b) A county government enforcement officer to capture and confine the animals.
c) The enforcement officer to notify owner if known.
d) Owner entitled to take away on payment of capture fees and custody costs.
e) Failure to take away in reasonable time after notice constitutes an offence punishable by a fine to the court or prison term and fees and costs payable to the County government.
f) Where owner not known, the captor may advertise within a specified time limit.
g) Owner entitled to take away on payment of capture fees and custody costs and costs of advertisement.
Draft a provision to be inserted into the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act (Cap 360) to address the concerns of the county government, noting to provide for exemptions in cases where the domestic animal is being escorted to an abattoir for purposes of slaughter or being taken to, or from a veterinary doctor or is a draught animal (draught animals are domesticated animals used primarily for pulling heavy loads or assisting in agricultural tasks and include horses, oxen, donkeys). (10 Marks)

The Republic of Yohana promulgated its new Constitution in 2010 thereby establishing devolved system of governance comprising of Nine (9) County Governments. Each county consists of the Executive arm of government and the legislative arm (County Assembly). Jiponde is one of the counties in the coastal region of the Republic of Yohana. Devolution included the transfer of certain functions from the national government to the devolved units. Water is one such function that was devolved to counties, enabling each county to set up a water company within the framework, of the Companies Act, a national legislation with application across the entire jurisdiction of Yohana. It applies to both national and county governments in relation to formation of companies, their management and dissolution. Under this framework, the County governor has the power to appoint and dismiss a chairperson to the board of directors of the water company whereas the county minister of water has the power to appoint and dismiss members of the board of directors. The dissolution of such companies is governed by the provisions of the Companies Act of the Republic of Yohana which at sections 894 lays out the only procedure and circumstances under which companies may be dissolved. Under this provision, the Registrar of Companies may strike off a company not carrying on business or not in operation. Some of the ways in which companies may be dissolved under the Act includes: Compulsory winding up by the Court, Voluntary dissolution of the company by members or creditors, and winding up of the company under supervision of the court. On 7th June 2025, the County Assembly of Jiponde passed a resolution adopting the report of the Assembly's Public Investments and Accounts Committee dated 3rd May 2025 on the financial statements of the Jiponde Water Services Company Limited (JIWASCO) for the year ended 30th June 2023. In its resolution, the County Assembly in essence resolved that JIWASCO be immediately dissolved and its functions taken over by the county department in charge of water services which was to henceforth undertake the operations of water services in Jiponde County. The County Executive oppose this move and its Director of Legal Services has approached the firm of GGG Advocates LLP seeking advice on the legal position relating to the unfolding events in the Assembly. Draft the appropriate document advising the County Government of Jiponde. (10 Marks)

Read the following excerpt from a legal opinion. The passage suffers from poor drafting and lack of clarity. Identify the mistakes linked to qualities of good English and drafting. For each mistake you identify and name, you should quote the specific phrase(s) that demonstrate the error.
"The employer's conduct in dismissing the client was obviously wrong and totally unacceptable and the client is extremely hurt and disappointed by this action which was done in a manner that was not in compliance with the law. And immediate reinstatement and a generous compensation are deserved because what happened amounts to unfairness, injustice and illegality all at one. The law is clear and settled that no employer should dismiss an employee unfairly, unreasonably and unjustly and in this case the dismissal was not fair, not reasonable, not justified and therefore the court must step in quickly otherwise the injustice will continue to hurt not just this client but also others. It is important to also point out that the client, who has worked diligently for more that fifteen years with a clean record, was dismissed suddenly and abruptly without any explanation, proper steps which are required under the constitution, statutes, common law, natural justice, fairness and decency were not allowed and the dismissal has caused a lot of suffering, emotional distress, mental anguish and personal loss to the client. This case is about fairness, legality, propriety, justice and morality, and it is clear that the employer acted in a bad way that was not proper, and the court must find in favor of the client because such conduct should not be tolerated." (10 marks)

instructions: Answer FIVE (5) questions, including question ONE, which is COMPULSORY. Question ONE carries 20 Marks. All other questions carry 10 Marks each. Attempt each question in the space provided. Answers MUST be supported by relevant statutory provisions and case law where required.

questions.questionText:
Tana International Sugar Company Limited (TISCOL), a sugar manufacturing company, entered into a sublease with the Cabinet Secretary for the Treasury (CST). The sublease is dated 10th November 2007. Through the sublease, the CST leased to TISCOL 20,000 acres (being L.R. No. 26554) of land in Tana County for purposes of sugarcane farming, milling and associated activities. TISCOL claims that the CST failed in its obligations under the sublease to grant vacant possession of the entire leased parcel of land. The land is occupied at several locations by squatters, which affected the implementation of TISCOL's business plan. In the year 2009, some of the squatters obtained conservatory orders in Petition No. 22 of 2009 restraining TISCOL from conducting any farming or other activities on the portion of land that they occupied pending the hearing and determination of the Petition. The conservatory orders remained in place for 7 years until TISCOL applied to have them set aside. This, TISCOL says, caused a failure of its business model, leading to increased costs over and above what had been projected. TISCOL says that its financial model was predicated upon the availability of the entire parcel of land, and the inability to access the entire parcel of land led to increased project costs, including additional loans and equity injections by its shareholders. As a consequence, TISCOL claims USD 200 million, being the additional expenses incurred over and above what had been projected in the business model. TISCOL filed its suit in the High Court on 11th November 2021. CST denies liability and has argued that TISCOL was granted vacant possession immediately after the sublease was signed. This, CST argues, explains why there is no correspondence from TISCOL in the first 3 years since 2007 complaining about squatters on the land. CST further argues that TISCOL has failed in its obligations under the sublease by failing to secure the land by fencing it to keep away squatters. CST has also alleged that TISCOL forfeited the sublease due to its failure to pay rents as and when they fell due. It was only after demand for the rents was made that TISCOL settled the outstanding dues. CST also argues that it was not privy to the TISCOL business model for it to be made liable for any losses or increased costs incurred by TISCOL.
Relevant Lease Excerpts:
Clause 7(a) "The Lessor shall ensure that the Lessee shall peaceably and quietly possess and enjoy the land without any interruption or disturbance from the Lessor or any person claiming under or in trust for the Lessor."
Clause 8(a) "The Lessee shall, at its own expense, maintain and repair all fences, boundaries and other structures on the land to prevent unauthorized access or encroachment."
Clause 10(a) "The Property is leased on an "As-is-Where-is" basis.
Excerpt from Mr. Tomlinson's Witness Statement:
"The government assured us that the land would be free from squatters and that we would have full access to the 20,000 acres. I had conversations with the CST and he gave me that assurance on several occasions. In fact, even after we took possession of the land, we continued to have the conversation with CST to have the National Land Commission (NLC) allocate us alternative land. He assured me that he would direct the NLC to make the allocation of the land. It is on the basis of these repeated assurances that TISCOL feels that the CST has breached the terms of the sublease. We relied on the terms of the sublease as well as the discussions I held with CST to make further investments on the property with the hope that the land access issue would be resolved. Unfortunately, it was not resolved by the CST. I am aware that there is internal correspondence between CST and the Chairman of the NLC where the issue of allocation of additional land to TISCOL was discussed and there were assurances that we would be given that land"
Excerpts of Examination in Chief of Mr. Tomlinson:
Plaintiff's Counsel: "Mr. Tomlinson, you have stated in your witness statement that the CST admitted to you that the government was looking for alternative land to allocate to TISCOL. Is it not true that you are aware of correspondence between the CST and the NLC Chairman discussing the allocation of alternative land to TISCOL?"
Mr. Tomlinson: "Yes sir."
Plaintiff's Counsel: "And you can confirm that you obtained this information from a contact who previously worked at the NLC?"
Mr. Tomlinson: "Yes sir."
a) You have been retained by the CST to represent it in this dispute. i. Prepare a Case Brief. (7 marks) ii. Prepare cross examination questions to be put to Mr. Tomlinson on whether there was an obligation by CST to grant TISCOL vacant possession and whether this obligation was breached. (4 marks)
b) You represent TISCOL in the dispute. i. Identify any trial objections you would raise and their legal basis. (3 marks) ii. During the course of preparing for the hearing, you establish that the NLC contact whom Mr. Tomlinson alluded to in his witness statement is Mr. Turnkey, an advocate in your firm. Identify the considerations that you should take into account as you advise your client on whether to introduce the piece of evidence obtained through Mr. Turnkey. (4 marks) iii. Prepare your response to an objection raised that the cause of action is time barred. (2 marks)

Mathew has been arrested and charged together with others for the death of his late wife, Melody. It is alleged that Mathew hired two people to ambush Melody as she came home. There is DNA evidence linking the accomplices to the murder. One of the accomplices, Lazarus, has entered into a plea bargain and has agreed to be a prosecution witness. It is alleged that the marriage has been laden with violence. Mathew is also said to have been cohabiting with Racheal, one of his secretaries, and Melody had confronted him on several occasions about the illicit affair. Melody had complained that Mathew was directing all the family resources to Racheal, leaving her and her children without much to live on. Melody had threatened to file for divorce. Mathew is a church elder and well respected within the community.
a. You are the prosecutor in charge of the case, what pre-trial advocacy will you conduct to ensure a watertight case against Mathew? (4 marks)
b. You have been hired as Mathew's advocate. Develop a potential case theory. (3 marks)
c. Describe the court etiquette you will observe as you represent Mathew through the virtual court. (3 marks)

After a six months' military tour of duty in Somalia, Mr. Kasoda was grateful to God that he had returned home to his young wife Alexa, safely. To rekindle their lovely moments together, Mr. Kasoda organized for a dinner date at the luxurious Gede Court Garden Hotel in Karen. The elegant lighting and sensual music created a romantic ambience for the reunited couple. As they enjoyed a vibrant platter of seafood, comprising of artfully arranged lobsters and colorful garnishes of citrus wedges, Mr. Kasoda noticed a tall frame of a man pacing towards them. The man made an abrupt stop at their table and gripped Alexa by the hand and demanded that she comes with him. The man appeared drunk and his true intentions could not be ascertained. Annoyed by the intrusion, Mr. Kasoda grabbed a wine bottle and knocked the unidentified man unconscious. The Hotel's security personnel immediately arrested Mr. Kasoda and surrendered him to the police at Karen Police station. After thorough investigations, a decision was made to charge Mr. Kasoda in court with the offense of assault causing grievous bodily harm. Having been retained by Mr. Kasoda to lead his defence during his criminal trial, you have collected Mr. Kasoda's statement, police statements, statements by eye witnesses and a video footage of the incident in readiness for the trial.
a) Prepare a case analysis that would guide you in your defense of Mr. Kasoda. (5 Marks)
b) Demonstrate five ways in which you would apply the psychology of advocacy to defend Mr. Kasoda. (5 Marks)

Ufanisi Millers Limited ("Ufanisi"), a flour milling company based in Thika, operated with a single large steam engine. In June 2024, the crankshaft of the steam engine broke down, forcing the mill to halt operations. Ufanisi engaged Swift Couriers Limited ("Swift") to transport the broken-down shaft to an engineering workshop in Nakuru town for repairs. Due to logistical challenges, Swift delayed the delivery of the shaft by ten days. As a result, Ufanisi claims it was unable to operate the mill for that period. The failure to operate the mill for that period is said to have led to substantial losses in flour production and supply contracts. Ufanisi has sued Swift for breach of contract, seeking damages for loss of profits for the ten-day shut down. Swift counters that they were never informed that the mill's operations depended solely on the return of the crankshaft. Swift also claims that the claim for lost profits is too remote to be recoverable. Ufanisi intends to call Ms. Ustadi as an expert witness. Ms. Ustadi is an industrial engineer and production expert. Her report concludes that the mill's steam engine could not operate without the crankshaft; that during the ten-day delay, Ufanisi would reasonably have lost KES 15 million in production value. The report also adds that a mill of comparable size as that owned by Ufanisi generally keep spare crank shafts to mitigate on down time.
a) Explain some techniques that you might use to address adverse evidence given by your witness during examination in chief? (2 Marks)
b) You have been appointed to represent Ufanisi in the trial. Prepare five (5) questions you would put to Ms. Ustadi in examination in chief (assume that the introductory questions have been asked). (5 marks)
c) Identify three key pieces of evidence you will require Ms. Ustadi to produce in court. (3 marks)

Contempt of Court greatly undermines the rule of law, O'Leary J. in the case of Canadian Metal Company Ltd vs Canadian Broadcasting Corporation observed that: - "To allow court orders to be disobeyed is to tread the road towards anarchy. If orders of the court can be treated with disrespect, the whole administration of justice is brought into scorn... if the remedies that the court grant to correct... wrongs can be ignored, then there will be nothing left for each person than to take the law into their own hands. Loss of confidence in the courts will quickly result in the destruction of our society."
a) What are the objectives of legislating on Contempt of Court in Kenya that address the concerns raised by O'Leary J. above? (4 marks)
b) Describe the Strict Liability Rule in relation to Contempt of Court and its application in Kenya. (4 marks)
c) In order not to suffer the loss of confidence in courts, under what circumstances may subordinate courts in Kenya punish for contempt of court on the face of the court. (2 Marks)

Mike has been sued for breach of contract and has hired an advocate of the High Court of Kenya to represent him in the suit. Mike is a lay man in law and states to his advocate that he believes he has a strong defence and is sure of a win. His advocate takes this opinion by the letter and as a result, has declined to accept suggestions of Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) that have been offered by the advocate for the plaintiff. He tells Mike that in fact a lawsuit will be lucrative as they will be awarded costs when the plaintiff loses the case. The case has proceeded and as it nears close of the plaintiff's case, his advocate has sensed imminent loss and has now approached the plaintiff's counsel to attempt an out of court settlement. The plaintiff's counsel has declined stating bad timing. Mike's advocate is furious and addresses the open court stating that the magistrate has been compromised for failing to order for ADR, since the Constitution of Kenya advocates for reconciliation and that he wants the court to recuse itself and that the matter starts de novo in a different court. In this moment of anger, Mike's advocate has also stated, in open court that it is Mike who made a poor decision by having false confidence in his defence.
a) Analyse three pertinent issues of advocacy in ADR that arise in this case. (6 marks)
b) Explain any two issues on trial advocacy in court arising from the fact pattern. (4 marks)
